comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of  Morihei Ueshiba, founder of the martial art Aikido and lifelong student of traditional Japanese occultism. Most people who know about him know of his extraordinary martial arts abilities; far fewer know that he practiced kotodama, the Japanese system of sound- and letter-mysticism, and was a longtime member of the deeply occult Omoto-kyo sect of Shinto. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

comments.title: Thankful Prayer
comments.content:
Because of your Sphere of Protection posts I have started night-time in-bed praying by imagining and giving thanks to the elements. The activity has been really nice and has improved my sleep and mood. I did not have a prayer habit prior to your posts. Because of a young child I am not imagining the rays and spheres of light, nor the concept of banishing.
What is the correct sequence of thoughts for a prayer?
Exempli gratia: being in the correct Mood, then addressing the Entity correctly, then giving Thanks?
What about sentences of Forgiving, asking for Forgiveness, and Accepting the future?

replies.title: Re: Thankful Prayer
replies.content: There's no fixed order for the elements of a prayer. The important thing is to find a sequence that works well for you and is received well by the beings to whom you pray, and that's best done through personal experience. You can do all the sentences you've described, in whatever order seems to work best; give it a try, and pray for guidance!

I have seven questions in all here. I’ve split them into sections, since many of these unfold from common themes.

I think I may have an idea for the cause of part of the massive shift in the 1980s. I think it may have to do with Neptune: I find that Neptunian influences seem much more negative with people who have Neptune in Capricorn or Aquarius compared to other generations. This might indicate that Neptune has two falls: one in Capricorn and the other in Aquarius. Both of these make intuitive sense to me, since Neptune, the planet of spirit would seem at odds with Capricorn’s very serious nature, and Neptune and Uranus are at odds with each other, so it would make sense for this to be the case.
One of the ways Neptunian influences manifests itself is illusions, hallucinations, and delusions. This describes what you’ve called the “vacation from reality” which we’ve been on, and which got under way in full in 1984: the same year that Neptune entered Capricorn. Thus it seems likely to me that part of what got that process going was strong Neptunian influences manifesting in a fairly negative fashion.
The delusion would continue on to the present, as part of the influences the planet has, not just negative, but as a whole as it traverses Pisces. Neptune does fairly often bring out delusions, and so it seems likely the delusion could continue: particularly given how intent many people are to continue to believe in the illusion.
This would also explain why the period immediately prior to Neptune’s discovery was marked by fairly negative Neptunian themes: Neptune was discovered in late Aquarius, and had thus spent most of the time it was coming into focus in Capricorn or Aquarius. This would also place Neptune’s exaltations in Cancer and Leo, which the planet traversed from about 1901-1929, before entering its detriment in Virgo.
What strikes me is that while there was an occult boom in general since the discovery of the planet, there was a fairly large boom of occult schools which seem exceptionally well designed, thought-out, and competently run during the first three decades of the 20th Century. This boom then collapsed, but it seems fairly dramatic for a planet moving into its detriment: a planet moving from exaltation into detriment though could produce just such a whiplash.
This raises three questions: First, does this line of reasoning make sense to you? If not, would you mind saying what doesn’t work with it?
Second, can you think of anywhere I could look for more evidence for or against this?
Third, if this is part of the reason why we’ve been taking our long vacation from reality, does this mean we should expect people to start getting much more aware of the reality of our situation when Neptune moves into Aries in 2025? I sincerely hope so, since it seems crazy how long we’ve been able to live in our hallucinatory bubble....

I also have another, unrelated set of questions: I started LRM a few weeks ago, for the second time, but in both cases ran into an issue: the work went well with lesson 1, but when I got to Lesson 2 found myself facing an issue. The first chapter of the book I chose to work with in the sentence exercise, Mystical Qabala by Dion Fortune, is drenched in the myth of progress. I despise the myth of progress, and so my mind rebelled against the sentence I chose.
This time, when I quit, I decided to jump back to Druidry, and so I’ve been practicing the SoP. This has felt remarkably good, and the ritual is fairly strong and powerful, but I recognize this as being likely part of the Watcher at the Threshold. I was initiated into the AODA several years ago, but never got anywhere with it; so this is in a way coming back to something I tried to start years ago, which makes it even more complicated.
My questions are thus:
Fourth, when you said it was necessary to learn to acquire information from sources you find repugnant, were my issues with The Mystical Qabala the sort of potential issue you had in mind?
Fifth, am I correct to see this as being likely the work of the Watcher at the Threshold? If so, what’s the best approach here? Is it to continue on with my AODA work until I’m finished, and then go back to LRM?

Sixth: Pagan Prayer Beads reads very differently from your other books. I like it, but it feels very different from your other works. Is there a particular reason why it has such a different style?

Seventh, I know you don’t do dream interpretation, but I have a question: I’ve had someone appear in several of my dreams for years now. We’re always in the forest, often singing and dancing, but sometimes just talking. She wears a very short white tunic, and looks fairly young (maybe 20ish), with hair the colour of gold. When we talk, she seems remarkably wise and thoughtful, and she has given me some great advice over the years. It never seems to occur to me to ask her her name during these dreams: I’m not sure why.
The reason I wonder if you might know what’s happening here is that the last time she came in my dream I saw the symbol of the AODA on her tunic. I’m not sure if this is new, or if this is the first time I’ve been able to remember it, but it seems to me that this means she is one of the gods of the Druid Revival movement, but I don’t have any good sources on them. Do you know who she might be, and could you point me to some sources which discuss her (or the Druid gods in general)?

replies.content:
1 & 2) I know of no other planet that has more than one fall, so you'd have to justify the idea that Neptune has two. That said, many astrologers consider Neptune to be exalted in Cancer and so in its fall in Capricorn, so that much seems accurate. It would be interesting to trace Neptune's cycle through all the signs and get a sense of what it does in each.

3) As soon as Neptune leaves its rulership in Pisces its power should wane considerably, so there's hope. ;-)

Those who are captives to their emotions and can't get past the offensive bits typically stop there; those who can get past that have passed a test, and have earned their way into the next level of the work. 6) Did you notice that I co-wrote it with another person?

7) I don't do dream interpretation, and figuring out the identity of someone you saw in a dream is part of that! As for the deities of the Druid Revival, there's some material in Ross Nichols' The Book of Druidry and some in Lewis Spence's The Mysteries of Britain.

Regarding Neptune:

Neptune is "no boundaries, no limits" planetified.

From 2004-2011, Uranus (technology) was in Pisces while Neptune was in Aquarius - the two planets had mutual reception. This is also the stage for the major adoption of social media; before that, it didn't really exist. The mutual-reception period was followed by Neptune entering Pisces itself, its domicile sign.

In that same period, the concept of "privacy" itself has come under attack, mostly through technological means. "No boundaries through technology" is evocative of a Uranus-Neptune mutual reception followed by Neptune in domicile through sheer social inertia.

What I would expect to see in 2025 when Uranus enters Gemini ("radical changes in communication") as Neptune enters Aries (the most me-first, individualist sign in the zodiac) is a resurgence of privacy, likely associated with a *reduction* in social media (and likely the Internet as a whole). You can't get much more revolutionary in communications than real-time conversation across the entire world.

comments.title: Geomancy Question
comments.content:
Hi JMG!

I cast a geomantic chart as a test a couple of weeks ago and have been thoroughly working through the details. The question was of little consequence, regarding recovering a long lost personal item. (Looks like I can get it back, but may need help from others.) If I'm understanding placements in the house chart correctly, I am in the first house and the lost item is in the second. My question is regarding the Company of Houses. Paired houses 1&2 don't have much going on (Fortuna Major and Amissio, respectively). Are all 2nd house questions limited to the pairing of the 1st and 2nd house (significators only) or do you consider houses the figures pass to or is this another time when passing into another house is required?

Thanks again for generously sharing your knowledge and hosting this forum!
_grey_

replies.title: Re: Geomancy Question
replies.content:
Okay, first, don't worry about company of houses until you've worked out the basic answer, which is given by whether there's a mode of perfection uniting the figure of the querent with that of the quesited, or not. Second, a lost object -- any lost object -- is in the fourth house, not the second. So see if there's a mode of perfection linking the figure in the first house with the figure in the fourth.

Third, if you've spent a couple of weeks analyzing a single geomantic chart, you're overthinking it. The answer in a house chart is given by the presence or absence of a mode of perfection between the significators, full stop, end of sentence. Everything else is just commentary on that, and half an hour should be enough to note the relevant factors and deliver a judgment on the question.

replies.content:

I see that later in The Art and Practice of Geomancy in the Special Questions section, you make it very clear that lost items belong in the fourth house. Thanks for pointing that out.

So with the correction and Amissio being in the fourth and second houses, there is a conjunction mode of perfection.

This brings up another question. Do you usually cast a new set of figures for a shield chart or decide ahead of time which chart to use and just do one of them for a particular question?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Okay, as a rule, if you use fictional deities in your magic you'll get fictional effects; if you want real effects, invoke real deities. There is a difference.

2) Here's the set I would use:

Air/East: Thor the thunder and storm god
Water/West: Njord the god of fertility and the sea
Fire/South: Freyja the queen of the Valkyries and warrior goddess
Earth/North: Frigga the queen of the Aesir and spinner of fates
Spirit Above: Odin, seated on Hlidskjalf at the summit of the worlds
Spirit Below: Fjorgyn the earth goddess, wife of Odin and mother of Thor

You could swap out Heimdall for Njord if you prefer, or have Tyr in place of Thor, but these are the ones I would use -- mind you, I'd pour each of them a horn of ale first, and ask their permission to invoke them in the ritual!

3) It's less potent, but that just means you have to work harder and practice more to make it work well. You can do it either way.

4) First, your ancestry does not matter. Let me repeat that: Your ancestry does not matter.. I know Shinto priests whose ancestors came from England, Cabalistic magicians whose ancestors came from Africa, and Druids whose ancestors came from literally everywhere on Earth with the probable exception of Antarctica. My suggestion? Hit the local library, find some books on mythology, read a bunch of myths, and choose a pantheon based on the myths that appeal most to you.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: If mystics don't do any ritual magic, including banishing rituals, does that mean they are relying on their patron deities for similar protection? Or do they use natural magic? Would they benefit from sharing a building with magicians so they would be in a space that is regularly banished?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may;

mystics are not a homogenous group. They have as much diversity as any other variety of practitioner. Evelyn Underhill discusses how some mystics do indeed practice magic, whereas others don't. For this reason, I don't see any conflict between theurgic banishing rituals, natural magic, and mysticism.

Going a little deeper, I'm not sure that all mystics even work primarily with a patron deity. Some mystics are devotional and could be called "devotees". Other mystics are more focused on ascending to the apprehension of the One. Is the One a deity or an impersonal ground of being? Can the One even care about mystics?

For lack of better terms, my understanding is that monist mysticism and devotional mysticism are very different in many regards. The devotee is in a passionate relationship with a personal being. The monist is ascending the greatest mountain to the very summit of experience. Frankly, placing both of these different practices under the banner of "mysticism" to my mind causes much confusion.

That said, if one is a devotee and passionately worships and works with a patron then the divine energies infuse out of that relationship and provide much protection. If one sincerely offers up their heart in prayer and love than the patron deity will, I imagine, almost certainly return that love many-fold. Deities have more capacity than mortals, and that , of course, includes the capacity for love, protection, and benediction.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The anonymous comment above nailed it. Some mystics practice rituals, including banishing rituals or the equivalent; other mystics use natural magic; still others use prayer, which is a potent technique with its own possibilities, very poorly recognized these days; and of course those mystics who have close relationships with patron deities do tend to be protected by their patrons. Mysticism is as rich, complex, and manifold as magic!

comments.title: Second Opinion on Keys to the Temple
comments.content:
Happy Monday,



I've been reading Dion Fortune's Qabala novel quadrilogy, and The Keys to the Temple by Penny Billington and Ian Rees as a study guide. In the last section, they've included an "interwoven light" exercise and several meditations on the materials which I'd like to try.

However, at the beginning of the practical work section they have a warning that the archetype they will be using as Guide is of course Fortune's own priestess character Lilith le Fay, who they say has become a controversial figure, having been ritually banished by the Society of the Inner Light and one other group, with "many stories of her malign influence". Billington and Rees feel she is still safe to work with in meditation, if given the respect she is due as a priestess of the Black Isis, and careful boundaries. They don't supply their own suggested SOP to do this work, just the interwoven light exercise, so they don't seem concerned for even us total newbs at the level of simple mediations.

Have you heard of these problems with Lilith le Fay in your circles? Is it possible that the archetype was contaminated in some way after Fortune's death? Or they they weren't getting le Fay, but rather an older Lilith archetype?

I have never done ritual magic at all, having a six and three year old, so I definitely don't want to mess with something Fortune's own society had problems with, but I am really drawn to this format. Just looking for a second opinion with more experience than me- I am gun shy about unintended contacts after some bad TSW episodes with ouiji boards as a teenager!

replies.title: Re: Second Opinion on Keys to the Temple
replies.content: I personally would not invoke Lilith le Fay as a guide under any circumstances, especially not without serious magical protections and the regular practice of banishing rituals! Since you have small children in your home, I'd recommend instead exploring the novels via discursive meditation using Keys to the Temple as a study guide. If you're comfortable invoking the Master Jesus -- that's the form of Christ that Fortune worked with -- you might consider praying to him for protection and guidance at the beginning of each meditation, to make sure no unintended contacts get opened up.

comments.title: Puzzled
comments.content:
Apologies for the length but a few examples for context...

I'm talking to a co-worker and she mentions that she thinks cardinals are so pretty but she never sees any. I say "what do you mean, I see them all the time". We go to a window, I point out a flock of 10 or more in just one tree (admittedly weird as usually they're in pairs) and say "see, they're all over the place". She gives me an appraising look and replies "Of course they are".

I'm driving through a huge, very crowded hospital parking lot with a friend (a nurse who works there) to pick up my sister after her cancer consult. I pass a few open spots and my friend began literally twitching and bouncing in her seat, saying we're not going to find anything closer, turn around and go back or we're going to be circling this lot for hours, those spots we passed are going to be gone. I say "I just want to see if there's anything right in front, because Mary's balance is bad and I don't want her to have to walk far". We get right across from the hospital door, there's an open spot and I pull in. My friend says "Things like this always happen to you and you don't even think it's unusual".

I'm driving in the early AM, exit the highway and as I'm going around the curve realize there's black ice and I'm going to slide right into the guard rail. I think "well I'm not going fast so it will just be a fender bump" and then the car slowly moves in the *opposite* direction of the slide, I miss the rail, the car straightens out and I hear a voice in my head say "Theeeeere you go! Now slow down!" in an amused tone.

I live in suburbia and woodchucks moved in a few years ago. Never caused much damage to my gardens until this year when they stripped the white mulberry tree bare-naked of leaves, demolished the potato beds right before they flowered, etc. In an effort to evict them I began dropping my dog's turds into the burrow and then filling in the entrance, reasoning that being fastidious creatures they'd move on.

Well the first day, we went 5-6 rounds of me filling hole, they'd dig it out and I'd fill it in again. Next day just once or twice, then a few days go by and the hole is still filled so I figure it's a success. Then it's opened up again. I say out loud "what the heck? Why do you keep coming back?" and then in a voice almost in tears I hear "but there's no where else to go!" And I realize that yeah, for wildlife my tiny yard is a little oasis surrounded by a vast desert of poison chemical-laced lawns.

Then I feel like a schmuck because the woodchucks are crying and in near panic, so I say OK, I'll go buy some rabbit food for you so you can fatten up on that, and any vegetation I mark (with a fence or by spraying with my diluted urine, ha ha) is mine and please don't eat but you can eat other stuff, we can share.

No response but the next morning as I'm delivering newspapers I'm planning my errands to include a run to the feed store. One of my customers runs out and hands me a $20 tip, which is weird because in 3 years he's not tipped me once, even at Christmas. And it turns out that 50 lbs of the high quality rabbit chow costs exactly $20.

So for about 10 days now I've been putting out food and water for the woodchucks and they've quit their rampaging. I do notice yesterday they're going through a LOT of pellets and think well this is going to get expensive. And I kid you not, this morning yet another customer who's never tipped me before hands me a $20 and in my head I hear "you're gonna need that" so I guess the expense of rabbit feed will be a non-issue.

So finally, here's my question. What do you think is going on here? You've mentioned that some people do magic all the time and don't realize it, but there's no intention or will from my end so I don't think it's that. It's more the opposite, I'm reacting to events or receptive, or sometimes just acted upon as in the black ice incident but as I don't feel any pull towards religion or worship it doesn't seem like a mystical path either.

It's almost as if I'm bobbing merrily along the river of life, no drama, oblivious to the things that preoccupy most other people (I'm definitely a Stoic!) but someone(s) is looking out for me making sure I don't get into trouble. Perhaps I'm too clueless to watch out for myself?

replies.title: Re: Puzzled
replies.content: This sort of natural psychism is almost always the result of serious spiritual practice done in your last incarnation. You have a choice; you can just coast along, and it will gradually fade out over this and the next two lives, or you can resume spiritual practice in this life and build on the work you've already done. Take your pick!

replies.title: Re: Puzzled
replies.content:

comments.title: Neil Gaiman and occultism
comments.content:
As you probably know, in Neil Gaiman's novel American Gods, the old gods derive their own level of strength from the strength of belief they attract in the present. Before American Gods was popular, I don't think I heard of neopagans using this concept, but it seems pretty common now.

Do you know who wrote about this idea before Gaiman? He must have got it from somewhere.

Have you read much Gaiman, and if so, what do you think about his portrayals of, and influence on occult concepts?

His works do seem to have been influential from the little I have noticed. I once met a highly paid consultant practitioner in a different form who mentioned something from Gaiman in much the same way as a Christian might talk about a story from the Bible. Though perhaps it was because Gaiman would be better known to the average person than specialist publications, and because, at least in the opinion of that practitioner, he illustrated the idea well.

Thank you again for these Q&A sessions, and for the time you spend answering questions from readers.

replies.title: Re: Neil Gaiman and occultism
replies.content: Terry Pratchett used the idea of gods getting strength from believers in Small Gods, but of course Pratchett and Gaiman had co-written Good Omens in 1990, a decade before American Gods. SO it is probable that they had somehow shared the idea, whoever encountered it first.  I am pretty sure I had encountered this idea before--but can't recall where.

replies.title: Re: Neil Gaiman and occultism
replies.content:
Given PTerry's early work, I feel pretty comfortable in saying that he got the idea from Neil Gaiman (who I suspect introduced PTerry to some variety of occult philosophy). There's a fairly major shift in PTerry's tone and themes starting with The Light Fantastic and firmly in effect by Equal Rites (whose wizardry/witchcraft split hews reasonably closely to the difference in capabilities between fantasy and actual magic!), and going by both authors' comments the period where PTerry was writing The Light Fantastic is when he and Gaiman first met...

(Neil Gaiman, of course, very much gives me the impression of being an occultist hiding in plain sight ala Carl Jung or Alan Moore; no idea as to the tradition, but I suspect chaos magic is involved.)

- pretentious_username

replies.title: Re: Neil Gaiman and occultism
replies.content:
Fascinating, thanks.

It's a long time since I read the Discworld series. A lot of people say that Equal Rites is when it starts to get good, so I didn't look too closely at differences between the first two books.

I'd thought that might be the case with Gaiman, and it's interesting to hear it from someone who, most likely, has more experience in these matters. Alan Moore isn't exactly hiding in plain sight, he's open about his occult interests. Though you have to wonder if the ones he discusses in interviews what he pursues in private, or a bit of deliberate misdirection.

replies.title: Re: Neil Gaiman and occultism
replies.content: I don't know who invented it, but it was all through science fiction and fantasy in the 1960s and 1970s -- Harlan Ellison referenced it in Deathbird Stories, for example. If I had to guess, I'd say someone came up with it back between the wars in the pulp era. As for its influence on modern pop occultism, no surprises there -- a lot of people in pop culture generally desperately want to see themselves as the most important things in the universe, and insisting that gods are dependent on their human worshipers is a great way to prop up the shaky humanego.

replies.title: Re: Neil Gaiman and occultism
replies.content:

comments.title: Life Sciences and Geomancy
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer and thank you again for continuing to host these Magic Mondays.

I’ve been practicing both the LIfe Sciences course and geomancy (from your book as well as some studies online, mostly via the Digital Ambler blog), and I feel like I’m making good progress on each. However, recently a very unusual circumstance came about. I was hoping you could help clarify this for me.

I had been focusing for some time on a new job in a specific field - a big change from my past experience and work. You’d advised me before to make the visioning as concrete as possible, so I did that, although I must admit the concretion was mostly around the idea of the job and the field and the manner in which it would help me move from my current one. I had been looking for new employment but at the time, all that appeared were more rehashes of the same job I already have. Suddenly, towards the end of the first cycle of Life Sciences courses, I was basically handed nearly exactly the job I’d been trying to vision, desire, will, and act into being. It came via a new acquaintance, and I had an offer almost immediately after beginning the interview process. So far so good.

Having given them my interest in taking the position and working out most of the specifics of the job I, somewhat belatedly, cast a geomantic chart for the question “should I take the job with company X?”. The chart cast with the mothers: Caput Draconis (first mother, on the right), Amissio, Puella, Amissio. In throwing the last figure, one of the dice was possibly misread as I had to move it to read it and I fumbled it in the process. I read what it landed on after the fumble.

The right witness was Fortuna Minor, the left, Amissio. The Judge was Conjunctio with a sentence of Tristitia.

On the house chart I interpreted this as a 10th house question, which held Tristitia, and denied perfection.

At first, I felt that this was horrifically negative. An ambivalent judge transitioning an externally provided fortune into a loss, with an overall outcome of sorrow.

Then I considered the situation: I knew I was taking a pay cut versus proceeding along the career path I had been on. I was looking for a stable job, quite explicitly. I was hoping for something that would give me a new beginning. And the question itself was rather loaded: I’d already effectively “taken” the job by saying yes, even if I hadn’t hard-signed the contract yet. I was also relatively emotionally bound up in the question.

Looking at it this way, the denial of perfection may be saying “you already took it, no you shouldn’t take it again”, Caput Draconis is favourable for new beginnings, Tristitia is in fact favourable in the case that stability is desired, and I knew I was taking a loss (amissio in the second house) on potentially more salary in exchange for the new experiences and hopefully better work/life ratio.

The part of fortune and the part of spirit were both in the 6th house, occupied by Laetitia. The Digital Ambler implies this could mean that the outcome is actually entirely in my hands.

Epilogue: since then I was able to negotiate for additional equity in trade against the lower salary, and I kept my word and signed the contract. I guess at this point I’m hoping to hone my geomantic understanding and I’m hoping that by applying Life Sciences to my work I’ll be able to help create a positive outcome in all respects.

So my question, I suppose is, how should I interpret this? Is the chart overwhelmingly negative? Is it clouded by my own fears / emotions around this opportunity suddenly arising and my still-uncertain confidence in the early days of practicing Life Sciences? Or is it telling me what I already know? And what does it mean to see Amissio as the left witness and fourth house - is it speaking only to the salary or does it outweigh other positives? I guess that it’s just a very confusing chart confluential with the extremely positive energy and high hopes I’d been carrying into it and I’m hoping to make some more sense out of it and what it might portend for my future with this firm and how I should interpret it in the story of a career arc.

Thank you very much again, and I apologize for the longwinded post.

replies.title: Re: Life Sciences and Geomancy
replies.content: As a rule, when you fumble a reading, wait two hours (or more) and cast the reading again. A fumble in a reading is a good sign that something has gone haywire and the reading isn't to be interpreted. I'd encourage you to discard the first reading and go ahead and cast a new reading for the same question, and see what you get.

replies.title: Re: Life Sciences and Geomancy
replies.content: Thank you very much for this!

comments.title: Past life patterns
comments.content:
A few thoughts just under the wire:

Above, in response to a question someone asked you answered “You have a choice; you can just coast along, and it will gradually fade out over this and the next two lives, or you can resume spiritual practice in this life and build on the work you've already done.”

One rather... disconcerting implication there is that no matter how much serious spiritual practice we do in this lifetime, any progress we make along the path can be completely undermined by a future incarnation who chooses not to do anything with the patterns we worked so hard to set in place for them... The idea of devoting a lifetime to spiritual work only to pass the torch on to someone who chooses to extinguish it sounds like a horrifying prospect. Is there anything we can do? Or is that choice always in the hands of a stranger?

That ties into a second somewhat related thought: If different spiritual systems have different aims and someone spends a lifetime developing proficiency in one of them, what happens if that spiritual technique or teaching dies out due to a cultural bottleneck before that person’s next incarnation with no equivalent to replace it? Would that person’s work in their previous life be useless or a liability in the new era they were born into? Or would the spiritual work they’d done allow them to pick up where they left off if they chose even if the available systems were different or incompatible? (Say, a Golden Dawn mage who reincarnates into a post-industrial society where the only available spiritual system is a shamanic technique that uses hallucinogens? Some of the older occult texts dealt with this by suggesting an unbroken line of mystery traditions going back to the dawn of time, and that would always be there... which was similar to your response to the person who asked what would happen if the sixth mass extinction took our species with it (you responded that human bodies would be there as long as they were needed)... but mystery schools and magical techniques are much more fragile than species (and species are pretty fragile themselves)...


-Eric S

replies.title: Re: Past life patterns
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, you're not passing anything to a stranger. You're passing it to yourself. You won't have your memories, but you will have your own character and orientation toward life -- and the more you practice now, the sooner you'll have the memories too.

Second, you've missed a crucial point, which is that mystery schools also have a dimension outside of matter. If there are people who need something like a Golden Dawn training, then something like a Golden Dawn training will be provided -- if necessary, by having somebody receive the teachings in a series of visions or meditations, and then pass them on to others. How do you think modern ceremonial magic popped up out of the woodwork in the mid-19th century at the moment that it was needed?

The universe is indifferent to our egos and their cravings, but it has its own agenda and isn't merely a random thing. You can trust it to do what it does.
